title: SIG 2021-28 Pandemic & Natural Disaster Reserve
text:
STANDARD INTERPRETATION GUIDELINE 2021-28
INCOME TAX – PANDEMIC AND NATURAL DISASTER RESERVE
This Standard Interpretation Guideline (SIG) sets out Fiji Revenue and Customs Service’s (FRCS) policy and operational
practice in relation to allowable tax deduction in respect to amount of funds deposited in natural disaster and pandemic
reserve account as required under section 28 & 28A of the Income Tax Act 2015.
It is issued with the authority of the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of FRCS.
All legislative references in this SIG are to the Income Tax Act 2015 (unless otherwise stated).
This SIG is in effect from 1st August 2021 and may need to be reviewed in the event of any relevant legislative
amendments.
CONTENTS
Purpose 2
Introduction 2
Legislative Analysis 2
- Pandemic Reserve 2
- Natural Disaster Reserve 4
- Natural Disaster Reserve Fund can be utilised for Covid-19 Pandemic 6
Appendix 8
1 | P age Back to Content
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

PURPOSE
1. The purpose of this Standard Interpretation Guideline (SIG) is to provide practical guidance on CEO’s
interpretation in relation to allowable tax deduction company is entitled for a tax year in relation to
amount of funds deposited in natural disaster or pandemic reserve account for the purposes of Income
Tax Act (ITA) 2015.
INTRODUCTION
2. Effective 1st August 2021, company which maintains a bank account as pandemic reserve account to
sustain the business activity of the company affected during the pandemic is entitled to a deduction for
150% of the amount deposited in pandemic reserve account.
3. If any amount available in the pandemic reserve account is used by the company when there is no situation
of pandemic or when company is not affected by the pandemic, amount of allowable deduction allowed
in respect of deposit will be included in the chargeable income.
4. Company which maintains a bank account as natural disaster reserve account to assist the company to
repair the damages caused to a building during the natural disaster in Fiji will be entitled to the allowable
deduction for 1.5% of the replacement cost of the building in respect to deposit made in the natural
disaster reserve account.
5. If funds available in the natural disaster reserve account is used by the company other than financing the
repairs to the building caused by natural disaster, amount of allowable deduction allowed in respect of
deposit will be included in the chargeable income.
6. Amount of funds available in the natural disaster reserve account as at 30th June, 2021 can be used by the
company affected by COVID-19 pandemic to sustain the business activity of the company during the
COVID-19 pandemic.
7. The examples used throughout this SIG are merely illustrative. They do not cover the infinite number of
factual scenarios that may arise. The relevant legislative provisions must be considered and applied to
each case on its particular facts.
That is, conclusions should not be drawn by determining whether the facts of a particulars case may be
analogous with particular examples, but rather on the basis of applying the correct tests established by
the law.
2 | P age Back to Content
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

LEGISLATIVE ANALYSIS
Pandemic Reserve
8. Effective 1st August 2021, company which opens a bank account in financial institution as a pandemic
reserve account to deposit funds in the same account in order to use the funds to sustain the business
activity of the company affected during the pandemic will be entitled to 150% of allowable tax deduction
in relation to amount of funds deposited in the account during the tax year.
Example 1
Company A opens the bank account in Bank A as pandemic reserve account in August 2021. In September
2021 company deposits $50,000 in the pandemic reserve account. Company’s tax year is aligned as
calendar year.
CEO’s Position:
Company deposited $50,000 in the pandemic reserve account during the tax year, company will be entitled
to allowable tax deduction of $75,000 [$50,000 x 150%] for tax year 2021.
9. Deduction will not be allowed twice, if company uses the funds deposited in the pandemic reserve account
to fund the company’s allowable expenditure to sustain the business activity of the company affected by
the pandemic.
10. Amount of funds used from the pandemic reserve account has already been subject to tax deduction in
respect to deposit.
Example 2
Based on the same facts provided in example 1, in tax year 2022 company A is affected by the spread of
COVID-19 and uses the funds deposited in the pandemic reserve account to pay rent of $10,000 and salary
& wages of $40,000. For tax year 2022 company claims tax deduction of $20,000 and $100,00 for total
amount rent and salary paid respectively.
CEO’s Position:
$10,000 of rent and $40,000 salary expenditure will be disallowed in tax year 2022 because it was paid
from pandemic reserve account, which has already been subject to 150% of tax deduction in the tax year
of deposit.
11. If funds deposited in the pandemic reserve account for which company has already been allowed tax
deductions and these funds are used in the business activity of the company, where company is not
affected by the pandemic or business activity of the company is funded when there is no situation
pandemic, amount of funds used from the pandemic reserve account will be included in gross income of
the company in the year in which funds are withdrawn.
3 | P age Back to Content
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

Example 3
Based on the facts provided in example 1, in tax year 2022 there is no situation of COVID-19 in Fiji, company
A uses the funds deposited in the pandemic reserve account to pay rent of $10,000 and salary & wages of
$40,000.
Company A makes no adjustment for the above transaction in tax year 2022.
CEO’s Position:
$75,000 was allowed for tax deduction in tax year 2021 for deposit of $50,000 in the pandemic reserve
account, in tax year 2022, $75,000 will be included in the gross income of the company.
$10,000 paid for rent and $40,000 for salary & wages will be allowed as a tax deduction for tax year 2022.
Natural Disaster Reserve
12. According to section 28 of ITA 2015, company which opens a bank account in financial institution as a
natural disaster reserve account to deposit funds in the same account in order to use the funds to repair
the damages caused to building due the natural disaster, will be entitled for a tax deduction in a tax year
in following manner:
i. in relation to a commercial, industrial or agricultural building, deduction is limited to 1.5% of the
replacement cost of the building; or
ii. in relation to a residential building, deduction is limited to lesser of 1.5% of the replacement cost
of the building or $500.
13. Allowable deduction in a tax year will be limited to replacement cost as illustrated in paragraph 12 of this
SIG, no further deductions will be allowed to limit the deduction to amount of deposit made in natural
disaster reserve account.
14. Natural disaster means windstorm, tidal wave, landslide or like catastrophe and reference is made to
definition of disaster under section 2 of the Natural Disaster Management Act 1998.
15. Disaster under section 2 of the Natural Disaster Management Act 1998 means a natural disaster and
includes the occurrence of a major misfortune which disrupts the basic fabric and normal functioning of
the society or community, or an event or series of events which give rise to casualties, and/or damage or
loss of property, infrastructure, essential services or means of livelihood on a scale which is beyond the
normal capacity of the affected communities to cope with unaided, but does not include man-made
disasters.
16. For purpose of section 28 (5) of ITA 2015 building is defined as structural improvements to land which
includes buildings, outbuildings and fence.
Example 4
Company B opens the bank account in Bank Z as natural disaster reserve account in August 2017. In
September 2017 company deposits $50,000 in the natural disaster reserve account.
4 | P age Back to Content
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

Company constructed a warehouse in 2012 which cost them $500,000. Replacement cost for the building
in 2017 was $800,000.
CEO’s Position:
In tax year 2017, company deposited $50,000 in the natural disaster reserve account during the tax year
for a commercial building, company will be entitled to tax deduction of $12,000 in tax year 2017 which
will be computed in the following manner:
Replacement cost of the building x 1.5% $800,000 x 1.5% $12,000
Company B’s allowable deduction for purpose of deposit in natural disaster reserve account will be limited
to $12,000 for tax year 2017. No further deduction will be claimed to limit the amount of deduction to
$50,000 which is the amount of deposit.
Example 5
Company C opens the bank account in Bank X as natural disaster reserve account in August 2019. In
September 2020 company deposits $20,000 in the natural disaster reserve account.
Company bought the residential property in 2015 for $700,000 to derive rental income. In 2020
replacement cost for the building was $500,000.
CEO’s Position:
Company deposited $20,000 in natural disaster reserve account during a tax year of 2020 for a residential
building, amount of tax deduction company C will be entitled to for tax year 2020 will be computed in the
following manner:
Replacement cost of the building x 1.5% $500,000 x 1.5% $1,500
Company will be entitled to tax deduction of $500 which is less than $1,500.
17. If company uses the funds deposited in the natural disaster reserve account to repair the damages caused
to building due the natural disaster, amount of funds used from natural disaster reserve account to repair
the damages caused to building due the natural disaster will not be allowed for tax deduction in respect
to repairs expenditure.
18. While expenditure is actually incurred to effect a repair is ordinarily a deductible expenditure for purpose
of section 21 of the ITA 2015, a deduction is not allowed as the expenditure has been financed by an
amount already subject to allowable tax deduction in respect to a deposit in natural disaster reserve
account.
5 | P age Back to Content
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

Example 6
Based on the facts provided in example 4, company B’s warehouse was damaged by a cyclone in January
2021. It cost $80,000 to repair the damages caused to warehouse and $50,000 was utilised from the
natural disaster reserve account.
For tax year 2021 company claimed $80,000 as tax deduction for repairs expenditure.
CEO’s Position:
Repairs expenditure of $80,000 incurred in 2021 to repair the building which was damaged by the cyclone,
only $68,000 will be allowed as tax deduction and $12,000 will be disallowed as this was already subject
to tax deduction in tax year 2017 in respect to deposit made in natural disaster reserve account.
19. If funds deposited in the natural disaster reserve account for which company has already been allowed
tax deductions and if these funds are used by the company to finance other business activity of the
company apart from using it to repair a building damaged by natural disaster, amount of funds withdrawn
from natural disaster reserve account will be included in the gross income of the company for the tax year
in which the amount is withdrawn.
Example 7
Based on the facts provided in example 4, in 2020 company B used total funds deposited in natural disaster
reserve account to purchase a motor vehicle which will be used in company B’s business activity.
CEO’s Position
$12,000 tax deduction allowed in tax year 2017 in respect of deposit made in natural disaster reserve
account will be included in the gross income of company for the tax year 2020.
Company B can claim tax depreciation in respective tax years for purchase of motor vehicle in tax year
2020.
Natural Disaster Reserve Fund can be utilised for Covid-19 Pandemic
20. Amount of funds deposited by the company in the natural disaster reserve account prior to 1st July 2021,
can be used by the company affected by COVID-19 pandemic to sustain the business activity of the
company during the COVID-19 pandemic.
21. Amount of funds withdrawn must not exceed the amount of funds available in the natural disaster reserve
account as at 30th June, 2021.
Example 8
Based on the same facts provided in example 4, company has not used the funds since it was deposited in
in tax year 2017. In tax year 2021, company B is affected by the spread of COVID-19 and uses the funds
deposited in the pandemic reserve account to pay salary & wages of $50,000. For tax year 2021 company
claims tax deduction of $50,000 for total amount rent salary paid.
6 | P age Back to Content
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

CEO’s Position:
$12,000 from $50,000 of salary expenditure claimed as allowable deduction will be disallowed because
$12,000 was already claimed in tax year 2017 in respect of deposit in natural disaster reserve account.
7 | P age Back to Content
A world class revenue service delivering excellence in revenue collection, border protection, trade and travel facilitation

APPENDIX
Income Tax Act 2015
[Section 28] Natural Disaster Reserve
(1) Subject to subsection (2), a deduction is allowed for an amount deposited by a company in a tax year
in an account with a financial institution for the purpose of providing a reserve for the loss to a building situated
in Fiji caused by a windstorm, tidal wave, landslide or like catastrophe.
(2) The deduction allowed under subsection (1) for a tax year
a) in relation to a commercial, industrial or agricultural building, is limited to 1.5% of the replacement
cost of the building; or
b) in relation to a residential building, is limited to the lesser of—
i. 1.5% of the replacement cost of the building; or
ii. $500.
(3) No deduction is allowed under this Act for expenditure incurred by a company in relation to damage to a
building to the extent that the expenditure is financed by an amount previously deposited to an account
referred to in subsection (1) and for which a deduction has been allowed under that subsection in respect of
the deposit.
(4) An amount for which a company has been allowed a deduction under subsection (1) that is withdrawn by
the company from the account referred to in subsection (1) and that is not applied to the repair of a building
damaged by a windstorm, tidal wave, landslide or like catastrophe is income included in the gross income of
the company for the tax year in which the amount is withdrawn.
(5) In this section, “building” includes structural improvements to land on which a building is located that are
used in connection with the building.
(6) Notwithstanding subsections (1) to (5), if a company has deposited an amount in an account with a financial
institution in accordance with this section prior to 1 July 2021, the company may withdraw the amount to
sustain a business activity of the company that is affected by the COVID-19 pandemic, provided that the
amount withdrawn does not exceed the amount in the account as at 30 June 2021.
Pandemic reserve
28A. (1) Subject to subsection (2), a deduction is allowed for an amount deposited by a company in a tax year
in an account with a financial institution for the purpose of providing a reserve for sustaining a business activity
of the company that is affected by a pandemic.
(2) The deduction allowed under subsection (1) for a tax year is 150% of the amount deposited by the
company.
(3) No deduction is allowed under this Act for expenditure incurred by a company to sustain a business activity
of the company that is affected by a pandemic to the extent that the expenditure is financed by an amount
previously deposited to an account referred to in subsection (1) and for which a deduction has been allowed
under that subsection in respect of the deposit.
(4) An amount for which a company has been allowed a deduction under subsection (1) that is withdrawn by
the company from the account referred to in subsection (1) and that is not applied to sustain the business

activity of the company that is affected by a pandemic is income included in the gross income of the company
for the tax year in which the amount is withdrawn.
